options.text: Having secured the funding, the researchers began their experiments.
Damaged by the flood, the building was condemned.
Arriving at the hotel, our luggage was lost.
Feeling nervous, he took a deep breath before speaking.

questions.explanation: Option (c) is a dangling participle: "Arriving at the hotel" implies the luggage was arriving, which is impossible — the luggage doesn't arrive on its own. Correct: Arriving at the hotel, **we found that** our luggage had been lost. Options (a), (b), and (d) correctly match the participle subject with the main clause subject (researchers, building, he).

questions.prompt: Choose the form that best shows the FIRST action: "She graduated last year. She is now working as an engineer."

options.text: Graduating last year, she is now working as an engineer.
To graduate last year, she is now working as an engineer.
Graduated last year, she is now working as an engineer.
Having graduated last year, she is now working as an engineer.

questions.explanation: Graduation happened first; working as an engineer is the current state. Use having + past participle to signal the prior action: Having graduated last year, she is now working as an engineer. Option (a) uses the present participle — implies simultaneous action (graduating AND working at the same time), which doesn't match "last year". Option (c) "Graduated" alone as a fronted past participle implies she was graduated by someone — passive meaning, awkward here. Option (b) uses to-infinitive — wrong for this sequence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a present participle clause to express reason?

options.text: Knowing the answer, the question was left for others.
Not understanding the instructions, she asked for clarification.
Understanding the answer, it was obvious what to do.
Knowing his way around, the map was unnecessary for him.

questions.explanation: Option (b) is correct: "Not understanding the instructions" shares its subject with the main clause (she) — she didn't understand, so she asked. Option (a): "Knowing the answer" implies the question knew the answer — dangling participle. Option (c): "Understanding the answer" implies "it" understood — dangling. Option (d): "Knowing his way around" implies the map knew its way — dangling.

questions.prompt: Reduce: "The policy, which had been challenged repeatedly, was finally revised."

options.text: The policy, having challenging repeatedly, was finally revised.
The policy, challenged repeatedly, was finally revised.
The policy, challenging repeatedly, was finally revised.
The policy, being challenged repeatedly, was finally revised.

questions.explanation: "Which had been challenged" is a passive relative clause — it reduces to a past participle: challenged repeatedly. Option (a) has an incorrect form (having + base verb instead of having + past participle). Option (c) uses the present active participle — wrong, the policy doesn't challenge anything. Option (d) uses the present passive (being challenged) which implies ongoing action — changes the nuance and tense.

questions.prompt: Complete: "___ with years of experience in diplomacy, the ambassador handled the crisis with remarkable calm."

options.text: Armed
Arming
Having arming
To arm

questions.explanation: "Armed with years of experience" is a past participle clause expressing reason/manner — because she was armed (equipped/endowed) with experience. The past participle carries a passive/stative sense: she was armed by her experience. Option (b) "Arming" (present active) would mean she was actively arming herself at that moment — wrong. Option (c) "Having arming" is not a valid grammatical form. Option (d) is a to-infinitive.

questions.prompt: Read: "She worked for twelve hours straight. She finally presented the solution." Choose the best single-sentence reduction using a participle clause.

options.text: Working for twelve hours straight, she finally presented the solution.
Having worked for twelve hours straight, she finally presented the solution.
Worked for twelve hours straight, she finally presented the solution.
Being worked for twelve hours straight, she finally presented the solution.

questions.explanation: The twelve hours of work came FIRST, and presenting the solution is the result. Use having + past participle: Having worked for twelve hours straight, she finally presented the solution. Option (a) "Working" (present participle) implies she presented the solution WHILE working — not the intended sequence. Option (c) "Worked" alone as a fronted past participle is passive — implies she was worked by someone. Option (d) "Being worked" is a passive continuous — not natural here.
